headline: US-Iran war enters Week 2 as Iran mines the Strait of Hormuz, Brent surges to $116.50/bbl, European markets crash on energy crisis fears, and SCOTUS tariff ruling triggers $175B refund scramble

top_stories.title: US-Iran War Enters Week 2 -- 140 Troops Injured, Kurdish Proxy Offensive, Ground Invasion Not Ruled Out
top_stories.summary: The US-Israeli war against Iran (Operation Epic Fury / Roaring Lion) entered its second week with no ceasefire in sight. The Pentagon confirmed 140 US troops injured, 8 severely, in the first 10 days of fighting. Defense Secretary Hegseth vowed the 'most intense day yet' of US strikes while the White House confirmed boots on the ground in Iran are 'not ruled out.' Kurdish forces are advancing into Iranian territory with US air support, opening a new ground dimension. Tehran is under sustained aerial bombardment with residents describing 'hearts shaking' and internet shut down nationwide.
top_stories.investor_impact: Extended military engagement with escalating intensity and no clear endpoint. Ground invasion possibility raises costs dramatically. US fiscal burden mounting from war spending plus $175B tariff refund liability. Defense stocks outperform but broader market faces prolonged risk-off.

top_stories.title: Strait of Hormuz Effectively Closed -- Iran Lays Mines, US Navy Suspends Escort Operations
top_stories.summary: Iran's IRGC began laying naval mines in the Strait of Hormuz, physically enforcing the closure of the world's most critical oil chokepoint. Simultaneously, the US Navy informed commercial shipping that Hormuz escorts are 'not possible for now,' leaving tankers without military protection. Shipping traffic through the strait has dropped approximately 90%. Insurance companies have withdrawn coverage for Hormuz-transiting vessels. Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted then deleted a statement about Hormuz. Mine clearance could take months even after hostilities cease.
top_stories.investor_impact: The most severe energy supply disruption since 1973. Brent crude at $116.50/bbl (+25% this week). Oil at these levels threatens recession in energy-importing economies. LNG supplies from Qatar (20% of global market) completely offline. Mine clearance timeline measured in months, not weeks -- this is not a short-term disruption.

top_stories.title: G7 Signals Coordinated Strategic Petroleum Reserve Release to Cap Oil Prices
top_stories.summary: G7 finance ministers discussed oil market stabilization, signaling the largest coordinated SPR release since the 2022 Russia-Ukraine crisis. The release targets the 'fear premium' embedded in energy markets. Market expectations are for a 60-100 million barrel drawdown. This is the first major multilateral policy response to the Hormuz crisis. Political pressure is mounting on all G7 governments as gasoline prices surge.
top_stories.investor_impact: SPR release could temporarily cap oil prices and provide 30-60 days of price relief, but cannot offset a full Hormuz closure indefinitely. US SPR already at historic lows from 2022 drawdown. Market relief likely short-lived without a ceasefire. Energy importers get breathing room but structural vulnerability remains.

top_stories.title: European Markets Crash on Energy Crisis Fears -- VW Announces 50,000 German Job Cuts
top_stories.summary: European equity markets crashed as the energy crisis deepened, with the DAX falling approximately 6% and Euro Stoxx broadly lower. Volkswagen announced plans to slash 50,000 jobs in Germany -- the largest single job-cut announcement in German automotive history -- citing sinking profits and Chinese EV competition. Senior EU officials admitted a 'nuclear blunder' in energy policy as the Iran oil crisis intensified pressure to reconsider nuclear power. The EU also bypassed Hungary's veto to transfer EUR 90 billion to Ukraine through an enhanced cooperation framework.
top_stories.investor_impact: European competitiveness crisis accelerating under dual pressure of Chinese industrial competition and energy insecurity. VW restructuring signals structural decline in Europe's largest industrial sector. DAX selloff creates potential value opportunity but only if energy crisis resolution is in sight. EUR weakness likely to persist.

top_stories.title: SCOTUS Rules IEEPA Tariffs Unconstitutional -- $175B Refund Liability, Section 122 Replacement Capped at 15%
top_stories.summary: The US Supreme Court's ruling striking down IEEPA-based tariffs has triggered a wave of corporate lawsuits seeking refunds from an estimated $175 billion pool. The administration pivoted to Section 122 replacement tariffs capped at 15%, a significant reduction from the prior regime. Senate Democrats introduced a bill to shield small businesses from tariff impacts. The ruling fundamentally reshapes US trade policy authority, constraining executive power over tariffs and creating fiscal uncertainty from the refund liability.
top_stories.investor_impact: Major fiscal implications from $175B contingent liability. Import-dependent businesses benefit from lower tariff ceiling. US trade policy now structurally constrained by judiciary. Global trade flows may normalize somewhat under the 15% cap. China tariff exposure reduced but not eliminated.

regional_summaries.Middle East & North Africa: The epicenter of the global crisis. Iran is under sustained US-Israeli bombardment with IRGC mining the Strait of Hormuz. Gulf states face a paradox: oil prices at $116.50/bbl should boost revenues, but Hormuz closure physically blocks their primary export route. Kuwait and Qatar are near-total economic paralysis with no pipeline alternatives. Saudi Arabia partially diverts via PETROLINE but capacity is limited. UAE's chokepoint exposure at maximum 1.000. Hezbollah escalation from Lebanon draws French military involvement. The IRGC declared Tehran will decide when the war ends. Russia caught conducting ship-to-ship oil transfers in the Gulf of Oman, profiting from the chaos. WHO verified 13 attacks on health sites in Iran as the humanitarian toll rises.
regional_summaries.North America: The US faces a multi-front crisis: war costs escalating with no clear endpoint, SCOTUS tariff ruling creating $175B fiscal liability, 5 recession warning signs identified, Dow down 800+ points, and the leveraged basis trade ($1T+) beginning to unwind in bond markets. DHS Secretary Noem fired mid-crisis. Midterm polls favor Democrats as Sen. Rand Paul warns of 'disastrous' midterms. The Fed faces a stagflation dilemma with its March 19 meeting approaching. Canada and Mexico benefit from USMCA exemptions and elevated energy prices. Trump hints at 'friendly takeover' of Cuba.
regional_summaries.Europe: Energy crisis fears dominate. European markets crashed ~6% as the Hormuz closure reignites 2022-era energy security concerns. VW's 50,000 job cut announcement signals structural decline in German industry under dual pressure from Chinese EV competition and energy costs. EU admits 'nuclear blunder' in energy policy. The EU bypassed Hungary's veto to transfer EUR 90 billion to Ukraine, setting a significant institutional precedent. Ukraine retakes territory as Russia's buffer zone strategy falters. ArcelorMittal's Ukrainian steel plant operating at a loss. UK deploys HMS Dragon to the Gulf. Central bank 'super week' March 16-19 will test the stagflation policy response.
regional_summaries.East Asia: LNG crisis from Hormuz closure hits hardest here. Japan and Korea remain the most exposed energy importers in the tracked universe. Japan's 10% energy independence and Korea's 70% Hormuz oil dependency create severe current account pressure at $116/bbl Brent. China vows to accelerate AI and technological self-reliance at the 'Two Sessions.' Hong Kong media tycoon Jimmy Lai accepts 20-year sentence, further eroding rule of law perceptions. China watching the Hormuz crisis carefully -- as both a major energy importer and potential diplomatic player.
regional_summaries.South Asia: India directly affected by the Gulf conflict with $40 million in export cargo stranded at Kandla and Mundra ports. India approved limited easing of Chinese investment curbs, signaling cautious diplomatic recalibration driven partly by need for manufacturing investment. Pakistan border tensions persist. Bangladesh heavily dependent on Gulf oil transiting Hormuz; oil at $116/bbl adds 1-2 percentage points to import bill as share of GDP. Sri Lanka drawn into the crisis after evacuating crew of Iranian vessel sunk by US Navy.
regional_summaries.Latin America: Colombia's leftist Pacto Historico coalition topped congressional elections, strengthening Petro's reform mandate. Mexico killed CJNG cartel leader El Mencho in a major security milestone, though short-term power vacuum violence is likely. Argentina's recession deepens at -1.3% GDP under Milei's shock therapy, but the country is becoming a net energy exporter for the first time via Vaca Muerta -- particularly valuable as the Gulf crisis drives prices higher. Milei is courting Wall Street with the energy export narrative. Brazil remains relatively insulated as a net oil exporter.
regional_summaries.Sub-Saharan Africa: West African fuel crisis emerging as oil prices surge. Nigeria and Ghana face spiking energy prices with limited fiscal buffers. Ghana parliament revived a controversial anti-LGBT bill risking Western donor sanctions. South Africa's marine gas oil price hit unprecedented highs from Gulf disruption. Gabon's IMF loan push reflects broader African return to Fund financing amid tightening conditions. DR Congo's political risk premium at 1,371 bps -- highest in the tracked universe. African nations most vulnerable to imported energy inflation.
regional_summaries.Central Asia: Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan are among the top beneficiaries of the Hormuz crisis. Kazakhstan's CPC pipeline route bypasses Hormuz entirely, delivering windfall oil revenues. Azerbaijan's BTC pipeline similarly benefits. These alternative energy corridors are attracting increased strategic interest from energy-importing nations seeking supply diversification. The region's relative insulation from Middle East conflict creates a growing comparative investment advantage.
regional_summaries.Southeast Asia: Chokepoint exposure remains high across the region. Singapore port congestion rising as trade rerouting adds volume and delays. The Philippines, Vietnam, and Thailand face imported inflation from the oil shock. Myanmar's ongoing state failure (military controls only 21% of territory) continues to depress regional stability. Indonesia faces energy import cost pressure with the rupiah under modest pressure.
regional_summaries.Oceania: Australia and New Zealand are relatively insulated from the Hormuz crisis but monitoring energy markets closely. Australia is a significant indirect beneficiary: LNG premium from Qatari disruption, gold mining windfall at record prices, and the Australian dollar at best levels since 2023 on commodity strength. New Zealand faces modest imported inflation pressure but limited direct exposure.

market_context.summary: Global markets in crisis mode as the Hormuz physical closure deepens the energy shock. Brent crude surged 25% to $116.50/bbl with analysts warning of $150/bbl if blockage persists. Gold shattered records at $5,195.60/oz on safe-haven flows. The Dow fell 800+ points with S&P 500 and Nasdaq also sinking. European markets crashed ~6%. Oil price volatility extreme with >20% intraday swings on conflicting signals from Washington. Bond market stress emerging as the $1T+ leveraged basis trade begins unwinding, recalling March 2020 Treasury market seizure. G7 SPR release signal provided brief relief but structural concerns dominate.

notable_moves.driver: Hormuz physically mined by IRGC; 90% traffic drop; insurance withdrawn; $116.50/bbl
Record $5,195.60/oz on safe-haven flows and geopolitical risk premium
800+ point decline on Iran war jitters, recession fears, and bond market stress
Energy crisis fears; VW 50,000 job cuts; EU 'nuclear blunder' admission
Broad European selloff on energy insecurity and industrial competitiveness concerns
Best level since 2023 on commodity strength: LNG premium, gold windfall, hawkish RBA
4.08% yield; basis trade unwind risk; Fed meeting March 19 approaching
Extreme >20% intraday swings; 13% drop on Trump de-escalation prediction then full recovery

watchlist_updates.summary: War Day 10+. Hormuz mined. Kurdish ground invasion. Economy in freefall. State approaching collapse. Internet shut down nationwide. Completely uninvestable.
War escalation with no endpoint. SCOTUS tariff crisis with $175B refund liability. 5 recession warnings. Bond market basis trade unwinding. Dow -800. Political stability declining.
Multi-front war: Iran strikes plus Hezbollah escalation from Lebanon. International condemnation growing over civilian strikes near schools. WHO verified 13 attacks on health sites.
No pipeline alternative to Hormuz. Near-total economic paralysis despite elevated oil prices. Chokepoint exposure at maximum 1.000.
World's largest LNG exporter completely offline. Hormuz mines block all maritime trade. Revenue disruption severe despite global LNG price premium.
VW 50,000 job cuts -- largest in German auto history. DAX crashed ~6%. EU admits 'nuclear blunder' in energy policy. Structural industrial competitiveness crisis deepening.
North Sea producer with non-Hormuz export routes. Oil windfall at $116/bbl. Primary energy crisis beneficiary in Europe.
LNG premium from Qatari disruption. Gold mining windfall at record prices. AUD at best levels since 2023. Geographically insulated.
Net oil exporter, geographically insulated from Hormuz. Commodity basket elevated. Structural positioning strong despite global risk-off.
CPC pipeline bypasses Hormuz entirely. Oil windfall revenues. Growing strategic interest from energy importers seeking diversification.
Recession at -1.3% GDP under Milei shock therapy. But becoming net energy exporter for first time via Vaca Muerta -- highly valuable at $116/bbl. Milei courting Wall Street.
Petro's leftist coalition won congressional elections, strengthening reform mandate. Oil sector policy changes more likely. Investor uncertainty on interventionist agenda.

data_quality_summary.notes: Full pipeline run with 2,963 pre-fetched records (World Bank + IMF WEO). Exchange rates fully refreshed via ExchangeRate-API (2026-03-10). Bond yields, equity indices, and commodity prices carried from 2026-W10 with reduced confidence (WebSearch/WebFetch unavailable during this run). 89 news events collected. 605 trend estimates across 55 countries. 6 escalations from GDP swings >3pp (RUS, ARG, IRL, IRN, KWT, LBY). 1 rejection (IRQ inflation). Iran economic data remains extremely low confidence under active conflict conditions.
